page: Hetzner Cloud cpx21 is a virtualized server instance configured with 3 shared vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC processor running at 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. The hardware allocation includes 4.0 GB of system memory, yielding approximately 1.3 GB of RAM per virtual core. Local storage is provided via an 80 GB SSD, and the instance operates under a QEMU hypervisor. No dedicated GPU hardware is bundled with this instance, and it includes zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Benchmark evaluations indicate a mixed performance profile. Single-core CPU performance is strong, with stress-ng single-core tests placing in the top 25% at 2667.29. However, multi-core scaling is more moderate, with stress-ng multi-core performance and Geekbench multi-core scores falling into the average and weak categories respectively. Memory subsystem performance is a notable bottleneck; PassMark memory scores are poor, placing in the bottom 10%, and uncached memory read/write bandwidth is weak. Cryptographic capabilities are supported by hardware-accelerated AES and SHA_NI instruction sets, which assist in maintaining average performance during encryption tasks.

Given the shared CPU allocation and modest memory-to-core ratio, this instance represents a budget-friendly tier designed for resource-dense, non-critical workloads. It is qualitatively cost-efficient for applications that require bursty single-core processing but do not demand sustained multi-core execution or high memory throughput. Suitable workloads include lightweight web serving, basic text processing, single-threaded utility scripts, and small-scale development or testing environments. It is less suitable for memory-intensive databases or high-throughput parallel computing.
description: Hetzner Cloud cpx21 is an x86_64 virtual server featuring 3 shared AMD EPYC vCPUs running at 2.0 GHz, paired with 4.0 GB of RAM and an 80 GB local SSD. Operating under a QEMU hypervisor, the instance delivers strong single-core CPU performance, as evidenced by a stress-ng single-core score of 2667.29. However, its multi-core capabilities and memory bandwidth are relatively weak, with PassMark memory performance ranking in the bottom 10%. The hardware profile lacks a GPU and complimentary public IPv4 addresses. This shared-resource configuration offers a cost-effective solution for lightweight, non-critical applications. It is best suited for basic web serving, text processing, and development environments that benefit from strong single-threaded performance but do not require high memory throughput or sustained multi-core execution.
